source.title: Placebo and opioid analgesia-- imaging a shared neuronal network.

confidence_rationale: Strong convergent evidence: PET imaging in humans shows that both opioid analgesia (remifentanil) and placebo analgesia share increased rACC activity and rACC-brainstem functional covariation. This directly grounds the cortical-initiation component of SD-021 -- the rACC/pgACC driving descending modulation via brainstem. Sample is n=16 male volunteers; effect replicated across opioid and placebo conditions, which strengthens the inference that the pathway is a general top-down mechanism rather than drug-specific. Limitation: PET resolution does not resolve brainstem substructure (PAG vs RVM); the connectivity measure is correlational. Overall high mapping fidelity to SD-021 cortical initiation claim.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Both opioid and placebo analgesia are associated with increased rACC activity and rACC-brainstem covariation; this shared network is not activated during pain alone.
mapping.ree_translation: In SD-021 terms, E3 commitment to a pain-accepting trajectory recruits a cortical (pgACC-equivalent) node that gates the descending inhibitory pathway. The rACC-brainstem covariation observed here is the functional correlate of commitment gating z_harm_s PE precision reduction.
mapping.mapping_caveat: PET cannot distinguish PAG from RVM within brainstem. The 'commitment' construct in REE (E3 planning horizon acceptance of harm) is broader than placebo expectation; placebo may not involve the same depth of trajectory-level commitment as voluntary pain acceptance during goal pursuit.
mapping.source_context: 16 male volunteers; remifentanil vs placebo vs pain-only conditions; PET with H2O15. Key finding: rACC-brainstem covariation present in both analgesic conditions but not pain-only.

confidence_components.notes: High-impact Science paper with replication across opioid/placebo; PET resolution limits brainstem specificity; placebo-to-commitment transfer is plausible but not identity
